sanskrit_devanagari:
दूरेण ह्यवरं कर्म बुद्धियोगाद्धनञ्जय ।
बुद्धौ शरणमन्विच्छ कृपणाः फलहेतवः ॥ ४९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
dūreṇa hy avaraṁ karma buddhi-yogād dhanañ-jaya |
buddhau śaraṇam anviccha kṛpaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ || 49 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; single danda terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.49।।)
IAST exact (dūreṇa hy avaraṁ karma buddhi-yogād dhanañ-jaya / buddhau śaraṇam anviccha kṛpaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.049 = MBh_06,024.049). Body text identical: 'dūreṇa hy avaraṃ karma buddhiyogād dhanaṃjaya / buddhau śaraṇam anviccha kṛpaṇāḥ phalahetavaḥ'. GRETIL writes 'dhanaṃjaya' (anusvara) where modern editions print 'dhanañ-jaya' (with hyphenation and palatal nasal); both are scribal-convention variants without semantic difference. Same for 'buddhiyogāt' (one word) vs 'buddhi-yogāt' (hyphenated).

anvaya.meaning: by far / at a long distance
from yoga (in compound buddhi-yogāt: from the yoga of discriminating intelligence)
winner (in compound dhanañ-jaya: 'O conqueror of wealth, Arjuna')
those whose cause-or-motive is (in compound phala-hetavaḥ: 'those motivated by fruits')

translations.verbatim_quote: O Dhanañjaya, keep all abominable activities far distant by devotional service, and in that consciousness surrender unto the Lord. Those who want to enjoy the fruits of their work are misers.

Seek refuge in divine knowledge and insight, O Arjun, and discard reward-seeking actions that are certainly inferior to works performed with the intellect established in divine knowledge. Miserly are those who seek to enjoy the fruits of their works.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, 248 chars of 1022-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One who has actually come to understand one's constitutional position as an eternal servitor of the Lord gives up all engagements save working in Kṛṣṇa consciousness. As already explained, buddhi-yoga means transcendental loving service to the Lord.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.49 Then again, O Dhananjaya, as against action performed with equanimity of mind for adoring God, karma, action undertaken by one longing for the results; is, hi, indeed; durena, quite, by far; avaram, inferior, very remote; buddhi-yogat, from the yoga of wisdom, from actions undertaken with equanimity of mind, because it (the former) is the cause of birth, death, etc.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as an explicit ranking. Karma performed with longing for fruits (phala-tṛṣṇā) is far inferior (dūreṇa avaram) to action performed from the yoga of equanimity (buddhi-yogāt). The reason: fruit-driven action is the cause of birth-death-etc; equanimous action is not. Therefore (atah) take refuge in buddhi — the wisdom of equanimity that arises from yoga and matures into the conviction about Reality. Those who are 'phala-hetavaḥ' (motivated by fruits) are kṛpaṇāḥ (pitiable, miserly) because they continue to bind themselves through their own causality. The verse is harsh on fruit-motivated action; Shankara does not soften this.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.49 All other kinds of action are far inferior to those done with evenness of mind, which consists in the renunciation of the main result and with equanimity towards success or failure in respect of the secondary results. The former associated with equanimity removes all the sufferings of Samsara and leads to release.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads buddhi-yoga as evenness of mind that consists in (a) renouncing the main result and (b) holding equanimity toward success or failure in secondary results. The contrast is sharp: fruit-attached action leads to saṁsāra (endless suffering); buddhi-yoga removes those sufferings and leads to release. The injunction 'buddhau śaraṇam anviccha' is read as 'live in (take refuge as one's abode in) buddhi'. 'Kṛpaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ' = miserable are those who act with a motive for results; the misery is structural, not moralistic — it is the consequence of being bound to outcomes one does not control.

positions.rendering: on the strength of Kṛṣṇa consciousness / devotional service
the intellect established in divine knowledge / divine knowledge and insight
the yoga of wisdom / actions undertaken with equanimity of mind
evenness of mind, consisting in renunciation of the main result and equanimity toward secondary results

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The compound buddhi-yoga is the doctrinal hinge of the verse, and the four traditions render it differently while preserving compatibility. Shankara reads it as jñāna-mature equanimity: the discriminating intelligence operating with samatvam. Ramanuja reads it as an operational discipline of result-renunciation. Prabhupada folds it into the bhakti frame as 'devotional service' (the Lord becomes the object of buddhi). Mukundananda renders it as 'intellect established in divine knowledge' — modern-devotional middle ground. The classical commentators disagree on emphasis but agree on the structural claim: action from buddhi-yoga > action driven by phala. This is what makes 2.49 the verse that ranks the two.

positions.rendering: miserable / those who act with a motive for results

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Kṛpaṇa carries two senses in classical Sanskrit: 'pitiable / miserable' and 'miserly / niggardly'. Shankara emphasises the sense of being trapped by phala-tṛṣṇā (thirst for fruits) — the kṛpaṇa is constrained by their own grasping. Ramanuja foregrounds the structural misery: those who bind themselves to results they cannot control suffer endlessly. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render it as 'miser', preserving the etymological flavor. The verse is tonally harsh — Krishna is calling fruit-driven actors pitiable. Engineering layers must not soften this.

literal_meaning: Action is, indeed, far inferior to buddhi-yoga, O Dhanañjaya. Seek refuge in buddhi; pitiable are those whose motive is fruits.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 2.49 explicitly ranks two stances. Fruit-driven action (karma motivated by phala) is dūreṇa avaram — by far inferior — to buddhi-yoga (action issuing from equanimous intelligence). The injunction 'buddhau śaraṇam anviccha' tells Arjuna to take refuge in buddhi, not in karma-as-fruit-engine. The closing tag 'kṛpaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ' is harsh: those motivated by fruits are pitiable / miserly — bound by their own causality to outcomes they do not control. Shankara reads buddhi-yoga as jñāna-mature equanimity that ends saṁsāric causation; Ramanuja reads it as result-renunciation that leads to mokṣa; Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds buddhi-yoga into devotional consciousness whose results belong to the Lord. All traditions preserve the explicit ranking. The verse establishes WHY one would adopt buddhi-yoga (2.48); it justifies the discipline by ranking it above its alternative.

cross_references.explanation: 2.39 named the two disciplines: sāṅkhya (discriminating-knowledge) and karma-yoga (disciplined-action). 2.49 introduces a third operative term, buddhi-yoga, that bridges them — the yoga of buddhi (discriminating intelligence) operating in action.
2.47 named the structure (right to action, not to fruits). 2.49 ranks the two stances built on that structure: fruit-motivated action is far inferior to buddhi-yoga.
2.48 defined yoga as samatvam (evenness in success and failure). 2.49 ranks action issuing from that evenness above action that lacks it.

The closing instruction at 18.66 (sarva-dharmān parityajya) reframes refuge-taking (śaraṇam) at the doctrinal terminus. 2.49's 'buddhau śaraṇam' is an early-chapter form of the same śaraṇāgati grammar; the engineering analog must not collapse the two.

remaining_gaps: Sridhara Swami and Vishvanatha Chakravarti commentaries available on bgus but not pulled into source pack.
